coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of ZnO/CQD nanocomposite using chestnut shell and evaluating its photocatalytic antimicrobial activity under visible light

Kang, Jun-Won
coredata.dc:description: This research evaluated the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity under visible light and investigated its mechanisms by synthesizing CQDs and ZnO from a single ingredient, chestnut shells, a food processing byproduct, and then wrapping CQDs on the ZnO to form a nanocomposite. Characterization confirmed successful synthesis, highlighting the potential to economically and sustainably produce two nanomaterials from one source. When CQDs were wrapped onto ZnO at an 8% ratio (8%-ZnO/CQD), the highest photocatalytic antimicrobial activity was achieved. The study demonstrated that CQDs effectively suppress the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, increasing the generation of reactive species, such as e− and •OH. The increased surface area from CQD wrapping led to the production of intracellular reactive oxygen species (iROS), further enhancing their antimicrobial activity and causing cell membrane damage. These findings suggest that 8%-ZnO/CQD holds promise for microbial control, offering a cost-effective and environmentally friendly solution to reducing water- and food-borne diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of (a and b) CQDs (d, e, f) 8%-ZnO/CQD, (g, h, i) ZnO and (f) size distribution of CQDs, while 8%-ZnO/CQD refers to the nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of 8%.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of (a) CQD, (b) ZnO and (c) 8%-ZnO/CQD, respectively. 8%-ZnO/CQD is a nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of 8%.

(a) Raman spectrum and (b) X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD) patterns (c) thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curve of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10). x%-ZnO/CQD; nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of x%.

Surviving population (Log CFU/mL) of (a and b) E. coli, (c and d) S. Typhimurium, (e and f) L. monocytogenes under (a, c, e) 405 nm blue light or (b, d, f) dark treatment. Data are presented as the mean of three experiments, with error bar indicating the standard deviation. x%-ZnO/CQD; nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of x%.

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectra of (a) CQD, (b) ZnO, and (c) 8%-ZnO/CQD, and UV–Vis- Diffuse reflectance spectra and Tauc plot of (d) CQD and (e) ZnO. 8%-ZnO/CQD; nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of 8%.
Schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of ZnO, CQDs, and ZnO/CQD.

Time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) decay measurements were conducted for (a) ZnO, (b) 2 %-ZnO/CQD, (c) 4 %-ZnO/CQD, (d) 8 %-ZnO/CQD, and (e) photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD with an excitation wavelength at 375 nm. Average fluorescence lifetime (τave) was determined using the following formula: (a 1τ1 2 + a 2τ2 2 + a 3τ3 2)/(a 1τ1 + a 2τ2 + a 3τ3), where a 1, a 2, a 3 represent the normalized percentage (%) of the first, second, and third components, respectively, and τ1, τ2, and τ3 represent the PL lifetime (ns) of these components, respectively. x%-ZnO/CQD; nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of x%.

(a) Effects on ROS scavengers on photocatalytic antimicrobial activity for Escherichia coli O157:H7, Salmonella Typhimurium, and Listeria monocytogenes treated with 8 %-ZnO/CQD under 405 nm blue light. Experimental values are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Different letters within the same pathogen mean statistically significant differences (p < 0.05). DMPO spin trapping analysis results of ZnO and 8 %-ZnO/CQD in (b) 5 mL ultra-pure DW of 2 mg/mL ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD for DMPO-•OH detection, (c) 5 mL methanol of 2 mg/mL ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD for DMPO-O2 − detection and (d) 5 mL ultra-pure DW of 2 mg/mL ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD for e− detection. 8 %-ZnO/CQD is a nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of 8 %.

Photocatalytic antimicrobial activities of 8 %-ZnO/CQD against (a) E. coli, (b) S. Typhimurium, (c) L. monocytogenes over three cycles of treatment under 405 nm blue light treatment. One cycle was 28.76 J/cm2 treatment. 8 %-ZnO/CQD; nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of 8 %.
BET analysis of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 8).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: BET surface area refers to the specific surface area (m2/g) of each type of sample. x%-ZnO/CQD; nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of x%.

Different letters within the same pathogen mean statistically significant differences (p < 0.05). x%-ZnO/CQD; nanocomposite in which CQDs are used to wrap ZnO at a ratio of x%.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of ZnO/CQD nanocomposite using chestnut shell and evaluating its photocatalytic antimicrobial activity under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Biotechnology, Dongguk University-Seoul, 32, Dongguk-ro, Ilsandong-gu, Goyang-si, Gyeonggi-do 10326, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Biotechnology, Dongguk University-Seoul, 32, Dongguk-ro, Ilsandong-gu, Goyang-si, Gyeonggi-do 10326, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research evaluated the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity under visible light and investigated its mechanisms by synthesizing CQDs and ZnO from a single ingredient, chestnut shells, a food processing byproduct, and then wrapping CQDs on the ZnO to form a nanocomposite. Characterization confirmed successful synthesis, highlighting the potential to economically and sustainably produce two nanomaterials from one source. When CQDs were wrapped onto ZnO at an 8% ratio (8%-ZnO/CQD), the highest photocatalytic antimicrobial activity was achieved. The study demonstrated that CQDs effectively suppress the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, increasing the generation of reactive species, such as e− and •OH. The increased surface area from CQD wrapping led to the production of intracellular reactive oxygen species (iROS), further enhancing their antimicrobial activity and causing cell membrane damage. These findings suggest that 8%-ZnO/CQD holds promise for microbial control, offering a cost-effective and environmentally friendly solution to reducing water- and food-borne diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: While water and food are vital for human well-being and ecosystem health, their contamination can significantly exacerbate the global burden of infectious diseases (Cissé, 2019). Photodynamic inactivation (PDI) has recently emerged as a versatile and effective method to combat microbial contamination due to its high and broad effectiveness against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria without promoting resistance, addressing a major drawback of traditional antimicrobial approaches (Fu et al., 2024). The core of PDI's mechanism involves a photosensitizer (PS) that, upon exposure to light, undergoes intersystem crossing (ISC) to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) that are highly effective in inactivating pathogens (Ho et al., 2021; Yuan, Wang, Lv, Liu, & Wang, 2014). PSs can absorb light energy at specific wavelengths depending on their band structure, making them suitable for applications such as degrading dyes and organic compounds, antimicrobial actions, and CO2 reduction (Karthikeyan, Arunachalam, Ramachandran, Al-Mayouf, & Karuppuchamy, 2020; Zhou, Yang, Tang, & Cai, 2023).

Due to its natural abundance, affordability, non-toxicity, chemical stability, and tunable nanometric architecture, ZnO stands out as one of the most promising photocatalysts (Valenzuela et al., 2020). However, since commercial ZnO nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) have a wide band gap (approximately 3.2 eV), they have the limitation of low photocatalytic efficiency under visible light, so several studies have been reported to reduce the band gap energy of ZnO NPs (Alberti, Basciu, Vocciante, & Ferretti, 2021; Kader, Rashid, & Omer, 2023; Khan et al., 2019). Furthermore, the rapid recombination with photogenerated charge carriers, susceptibility to photo-corrosion, and tendency for agglomeration pose significant challenges when using ZnO for water and food safety (Al Ja'farawy, Purwanto, & Widiyandari, 2022; Muthulingam, Bae, Khan, Lee, & Uthirakumar, 2016). Various methods have been investigated to mitigate these limitations, including the addition of dopants, the formation of heterojunctions, and the integration of carbon materials (Che et al., 2023).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel carbon-based nanomaterial discovered in 2004, exhibit unique properties such as small size (<10 nm), low toxicity, luminescence, water solubility, thermal stability, and ease of functionalization (Kurian & Paul, 2021). Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs stand out due to their lack of toxic heavy metals, such as cadmium or lead, which are common in conventional quantum dots (Saadh et al., 2024). This makes CQDs more environmentally sustainable and reduces health risks associated with their use (Devi, Rajput, Thakur, Kim, & Kumar, 2019). Thus, CQDs are versatile nanomaterials suitable for applications in biolabeling, sensing, drug delivery, energy conversion, and catalysis (Kurian & Paul, 2021). The fact that CQDs can be synthesized from any carbon-containing starting materials highlights their potential for utilizing byproducts from food supply, thus offering a sustainable approach to their synthesis (Huang, Hung, Weng, Chen, & Lai, 2019). Besides CQDs, metallic NPs can also be synthesized using a green synthesis. Specifically, different biological sources, including plant extracts, microorganisms, and enzymes, can be employed to synthesize metallic NPs by reducing metal ions within the solution (Singh, Singh, & Rawat, 2019). In particular, the synthesis of metallic NPs occurs by utilizing plant extracts from various sources such as leaves, roots, bark, and fruits as the rich composition of biomolecules and phytochemicals within these extracts, including polyphenols, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, serve as both reducing and stabilizing agents for metal ions during the synthesis process (Verma, Pathak, Srivastava, Prawer, & Tomljenovic-Hanic, 2021). This implies the potential beneficial utilization of food processing byproducts in the synthesis of ZnO as well. In particular, green synthesis can be considered a viable approach to enhancing the photocatalytic activity of ZnO NPs, as green-synthesized ZnO NPs exhibit various improved properties, such as smaller band gap energy, compared to commercial ZnO NPs due to surface modification induced by components within plant extracts (Khan et al., 2019; Soto-Robles et al., 2019). Lately, the combination of carbon materials with ZnO has gained significant attention for their function as photoelectron reservoirs that capture and transfer photogenerated electrons from ZnO to various substrates (Al Ja'farawy et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2021; Mandal, Paul, Datta, & Jana, 2021; Muthulingam et al., 2016; Muthulingam, Lee, & Uthirakumar, 2015), so wrapping CQD on the ZnO surface could improve photocatalytic efficiency further.

Chestnut is a valuable fruit consumed fresh or processed into frozen nuts and derived products, with global production reaching approximately 2.3 million tons in 2020 (Kocer et al., 2024; Pinto et al., 2023). During the chestnut peeling process, non-commercially valuable shells are generated as byproducts, making up about 10–15 % of the total weight and leading to significant environmental issues (Shen et al., 2023). However, these byproducts are rich in polyphenols, including hydrolysable tannins with antioxidant properties (Sorice et al., 2016; Youn et al., 2016). In addition, chestnut shells have been used in studies on activated carbon, biofuels, and carbon nanotubes as porous carbon precursors for energy storage applications (Januszewicz et al., 2020; Rodríguez-Sánchez et al., 2019; Yang, Sun, Li, Chen, & Tang, 2020). These characteristics, specifically their high polyphenol and carbon content, indicate that chestnut shells are suitable for synthesizing both ZnO and CQDs, facilitating the fabrication of ZnO/CQD nanocomposites from a single type of food byproduct.

In this study, chestnut shell byproduct was repurposed for CQD production after its initial use in ZnO NP synthesis, maximizing its functional application by utilizing the remaining residue. This dual-utilization strategy highlights the efficient use of food byproducts by enabling the synthesis of two types of nanoparticles from a single source, reducing waste while achieving multifunctionality. However, research employing this dual approach remains largely unexplored. Therefore, this study proposes a pioneering dual-utilization strategy that first extracts polyphenol components from chestnut shells for the synthesis of ZnO and then reuses the remaining residue to produce CQDs. These nanoparticles are subsequently combined into ZnO/CQD nanocomposites designed to exhibit photocatalytic activity under visible light, thereby enabling efficient material usage while achieving a photocatalyst with enhanced functionality.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of these nanocomposites against pathogenic bacteria (Escherichia coli O157:H7, Salmonella Typhimurium, Listeria monocytogenes) was evaluated for different ratios of CQDs decorating ZnO to determine the optimal synthesis conditions for the highest photocatalytic activity. Finally, we elucidated the underlying mechanisms contributing to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the ZnO/CQD nanocomposite by incorporating CQDs into ZnO, providing a foundation for future research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO, CQD, and x%- ZnO/CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Chestnut shells (Castanea crenata) were purchased from Incha (Seoul, South Korea) and ground using a grinder. Ten grams of chestnut shell powder were extracted with 100 mL of ultrapure distilled water (DW) at 100℃ for 40 min. Extracts were then filtered through a Whatman No. 1 filter paper (Advantec, Tokyo, Japan). Filtered chestnut shell residue was dried in a drying oven at 50 °C for 24 h.

For synthesizing ZnO NPs, the chestnut shell extract was used as a reducing agent and capping agent to react with zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2; Daejung, Siheung, South Korea) to form ZnO NPs. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (two grams) was added to 40 mL of the chestnut shell extract and the solution was stirred at 80 °C for 1 h to reduce zinc ions and stabilize NPs (Hamed, Obeid, & Abu-Huwaij, 2023). The solution was then calcinated in a muffle furnace at 400 °C for 1 h to produce pure ZnO NP by removing hydroxyl group (Osuntokun, Onwudiwe, & Ebenso, 2019).

CQDs were synthesized using chestnut shell extraction residue as carbon source. Ultrasonication (Ultrasonic Homogenizer; UMC, UH-700Z, 8L21013G) was performed for 5 min to well disperse 5 g of chestnut shell extraction residue in 50 mL of ultrapure DW. The solution was then reacted in a Teflon autoclave at a temperature of 200 °C for 24 h. The supernatant was filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 μm) to remove trace amounts of insoluble impurities. Dialysis was then performed for 2 d using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500 – 1000 Da) to remove unreacted residues and macromolecular compounds, during which the ultrapure DW was replaced every 12 h (Janus, Radwan-Pragłowska, Piątkowski, & Bogdał, 2020; Zhan et al., 2018). The dialyzed solution was freeze dried using a freeze dryer (UK4112, ilShinBioBase, Korea). Obtained CQD powder was dissolved in DW at a concentration of 20 mg/mL to make a stock solution, with the initial pH of the solution being approximately 2–3. To prevent any adverse effects on microorganisms, the pH adjusted to 7 using 1 M HCl and NaOH.

ZnO NPs and CQDs were mixed to synthesize a ZnO/CQD nanocomposite. Typically, 0.4 g of ZnO was added to a 200 mL beaker, and a 2 mg/mL CQD solution was added in varying volumes according to desired wrapping ratio: 4 mL for 2 %-ZnO/CQD, 8 mL for 4 %-ZnO/CQD, 12 mL for 6 %-ZnO/CQD, 16 mL for 8 %-ZnO/CQD, and 20 mL for 10 %-ZnO/CQD. DW was added to adjust the total reaction volume to 30 mL. The mixture was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 4 h. The suspension was then placed in a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner. The autoclave was heated and stored in air dry oven at 140 °C for 4 h. White precipitate was then washed three times to remove nonreacted compounds with ultrapure DW. Obtained ZnO/CQD nanocomposites were dried in a dry oven at 50 °C for 24 h and then pulverized into powder. Proportions of CQDs in the complex were 2 %, 4 %, 6 %, 8 %, and 10 %, respectively. Corresponding nanocomposite was referred to as x%-ZnO/CQD.

CQDs, ZnO, and x%-ZnO/CQD of nanocomposites were characterized using various analytical instruments. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) micrographs of ZnO, CQDs and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 8) morphology were obtained using FE-TEM (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). Particle size distribution of CQDs was determined through dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Zetasizer Nano° ZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK). Chemical structures of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 8) were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Versaprobe Ⅱ, ULVAC-PHI, Japan). Detailed band structures of ZnO, CQDs, and 8 %-ZnO/CQD were analyzed using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS; Versaprobe Ⅱ, ULVAC-PHI, Japan) and UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–Vis-DRS) analysis of ZnO and CQD was conducted using UV–vis spectrometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). Crystal phase and compositions of nanocomposites were recorded on an X-Ray Diffractometer (Ultima Ⅳ, Rigaku, Japan) with monochromatized Cu-Kα (λ = 1.5409 Å) radiation in the range from 10° to 100° at a scan speed of 3°/min and data dot of 0.02°/sec. Raman spectra were recorded at room temperature with a 532 nm Laser Raman spectrometer (Jobin Yvon, LabRam HR Evolution, HORIBA, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and fluorescence lifetime of nanocomposites were recorded using a spectrofluorophotometer (F-7100, 5 J1-0044, HITACHI, Japan). PL lifetime of nanocomposites was measured using a confocal microscope (MircroTime-200, Picoquant, Germany) and a single-mode pulsed diode laser (375 nm with ps pulse width and average power of 0.2–5 μW) operating in 0.5 & 1 MHz for ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 8). Symphotime-64 software (Ver.2.2) by the exponential decay model was used for obtained PL decays. Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was performed using electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR; EMXplus-9.5/12/P/L System, Bruker, Germany). To detect signals such as superoxide and hydroxyl radical, 20 μL of 10 M DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide; Tokyo chemical industry) was mixed 10 mg of ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD. Similarly, to detect electrons, 1 μmol/L TEMPO (2,2,6,6,-Tetramethylpiperidine 1-oxyl, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was mixed with 10 mg of ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD. Each mixture was then treated with 405 nm blue light at a dose of 23.75 J/cm2 after adding 5 mL of DW or methanol for DMPO and 5 mL of DW for TEMPO. At this time, light control samples containing only DMPO or TEMPO with their respective solvents, as well as dark control samples without 405 nm blue light treatment, were prepared. After treatment, sample was filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 μm) to remove nanoparticles. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was performed using a surface area analyzer (BELSORP-MAX, BEL, Japan). Thermal stability was recorded using a thermogravimetric analyzer (SDT Q600, TA Instruments, USA). Analysis conditions were: N2 as atmosphere gas, a temperature range of 30 – 800°C, and a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

Three strains each of E. coli O157:H7 (ATCC 43890, ATCC 43889, and ATCC 35150), S. Typhimurium (DT 104, ATCC 19585, and ATCC 19115), and L. monocytogenes (ATCC 15313, ATCC 27649, and ATCC 25923) were provided by the Food Microbial Laboratory at Dongguk University (Gyeonggi-do, Republic of Korea) for use in this study. All strains were cultured individually in 5 mL of tryptic soy broth (TSB; MBcell, Seoul, Korea) at 37 °C for 24 h. Cultured cells were harvested by centrifugation at 4,500 rpm for 20 min. After washing twice with phosphate buffer saline (PBS), the final pellet was resuspended in PBS. Suspended solutions of all pathogens were combined to form a mixed culture cocktail containing approximately 107-108 CFU/mL of each pathogen. This culture cocktail was used as the inoculum in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial effect of CQD, ZnO, x%- ZnO/CQD under 405 nm blue light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: A constant electric current (1.02 A) through a DC power supply (TDP-1001B, TOYOTECH, Korea) was used to activate four blue LED chips (LG Innotek Co., Seoul, Korea) emitting 405 nm wavelength. The vertical distance between the blue LED module and the optical probe was 5 cm. The intensity of the module was measured using a spectrometer (AvaSpec-ULS2048-USBW-UA-50; Avantes, Apeldoorn, Netherlands) with a calibration range of 360–1100 nm. The entire range of emitted irradiation was integrated and calculated as 31.66 mW/cm2 (Kim, Shin, Kim, & Kang, 2022).

Samples were prepared by placing 5 mL of distilled water containing each CQDs, ZnO, and x%-ZnO/CQD at a concentration of 2 mg/mL and 0.1 mL of inoculum in a Petri-dish (60 mm * 15 mm). After closing the lid, the Petri-dish was placed at a vertical distance of 5 cm from the blue light source and treated with a dose of 9.59–57.51 J/cm2. In this study, a light-control was prepared not incorporating nanocomposites treated with 405 nm blue light and a dark-control was prepared incorporating nanocomposites without 405 nm blue light treatment. At this time, 28.76 J/cm2 treatment was carried out in one cycle. This cycle was repeated three times using 8 %-ZnO/CQD to evaluate reusability of nanocomposites. The 8 %-ZnO/CQD was harvested by centrifugation at 10,000 g after treatment with one cycle intervals and added 5 ml of distilled water to remove ROS bias.

After treatment, a 1 mL aliquot of the treated sample was subjected to a series of 10-fold dilutions using 9 mL of sterile 0.2 % peptone water (PW; peptone water (NIER), MBcell, Seoul, Korea). Subsequently, 0.1 mL of the resulting dilution was spread onto selective media and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Sorbitol MacConkey Agar (MBcell, Seoul, Korea), Xylose Lysine deoxycholate agar (MBcell, Seoul, Korea), and Oxford agar base with Oxford Listeria selective supplement (MBcell, Seoul, Korea) were used as selective media for enumerating E. coli, S. Typhimurium, and L. monocytogenes, respectively. After incubation, colonies of typical form were counted and calculated as log10 CFU/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of intracellular reactive oxygen species (iROS), occurrence of lipid peroxidation, and destruction of cell membranes
ce:section.ce:para: CM-H2DCFDA [5-(and-6)- chloromethyl-2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate; Invitrogen], propidium iodide (PI; Sigma Aldrich), and diphenyl-1-pyrenylphosphine (DPPP; aladdin) as fluorescent dyes were used to quantitatively evaluate generation of intracellular ROS (iROS), destruction of cell membranes and incidence of lipid peroxidation in cell membranes.

Five milliliters of DW containing CQD, ZnO, or x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 8) at a concentration of 2 mg/mL inoculated with 0.1 mL inoculum obtained an optical density of 0.3 at 600 nm (OD600 = 0.3) was treated with 405 nm blue light at a dose of 23.75 J/cm2 (E. coli, S. Typhimurium) or 28.49 J/cm2 (L. monocytogenes) at room temperature via pipetting at 5 min intervals. During this process, a light-control was prepared by not incorporating nanocomposites treated with 405 nm blue light and a dark-control was prepared by incorporating nanocomposites without 405 nm blue light treatment. After treatment, cells were harvested by centrifugation at 10,000 g for 10 min and washed twice with PBS to remove media and impurities. The final cell pellet was incubated with CM-H2DCFDA, PI, or DPPP dye solutions at a concentration of 5 μM, 2.9 μM, or 50 μM for 15 min, 10 min, or 20 min or at 37 °C. After incubation, cells were centrifuged to harvest reacted cells and washed twice with PBS to remove remaining dye that did not react with cells. Fluorescence was measured with a spectrofluorophotometer (F-7100, HITACHI, Japan) at excitation/emission wavelengths of 495/520 nm for CM-H2DCFDA, 493/630 nm for PI, and 351/380 nm for DPPP. Generation of iROS, destruction of cell membrane, and incidence of lipid peroxidation were calculated as follows: [(Fluorescence value of 405 nm blue light treated cells) / (Fluorescence value of dark-treated cells)].

Data of antimicrobial effects of nanocomposites under blue or dark treatment, scavenging treatment, the occurrence of destruction and lipid peroxidation in the cell membrane, and the value of iROS generation were replicated three times. All statistical analysis were performed using analysis of variance and Kruskal-Wallis test. The Bonferroni test was performed as a post-hoc when Kruskal-Wallis test results were statistically significant. For all cases, P-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All statistical analyses were performed using Statistical Analysis System (SAS) Version 9.4 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQD, ZnO, and x%-ZnO/CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a and b show HR-TEM images of CQDs, which appear spherical with lattice structures, consistent with CQDs synthesized via green methods (Malavika et al., 2021; Mindivan & Göktaş, 2023; Zhu, Shen, Wang, & Luo, 2021). Thus, the CQDs synthesized from chestnut shells exhibit a similar morphology and lattice structure to those reported in other studies. Fig. 1a indicates that the CQDs have a size of approximately 4–6 nm, while Fig. 1b shows CQDs with a size of approximately 12 nm. Additionally, the lattice spacing of 0.36 nm corresponds to the graphite (0 0 2) plane (Kaur, Bhattacharya, & Maity, 2024; Nammahachak et al., 2022). Fig. 1c shows the size distribution of CQDs determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS), which measures the size of particles based on scattered light in a solution (Gorbenko & Trusova, 2011; Mukherjee & Sasikala, 2013). The particle size range of CQDs were 5 – 13.5 nm and the average size was 7.66 nm (Fig. 1c). These results indicate that the CQDs synthesized in this study not only align with the commonly known size of CQDs (approximately 10 nm) (Wang & Hu, 2014), but also matches the size of CQDs identified in HR-TEM images (Fig. 1.a-b). Fig. 1d-f illustrates that the ZnO NPs, while Fig. 1g-i illustrates that the 8 %-ZnO/CQD. The ZnO NPs observed in Fig. 1d-f exhibit a rectangular shape, with their longer dimensions ranging from approximately 13 to 20 nm (Fig. 1d). The lattice spacings in Fig. 1f and Fig. 1i correspond to the hexagonal (110) plane at 0.22 nm, confirming the presence of ZnO NPs (Hallam et al., 2021). Several studies have shown that ZnO synthesized using green synthesis can exhibit diverse morphologies, including semispherical (Davar, Majedi, & Mirzaei, 2015), spherical (Thi et al., 2020), rod (Nagajyothi, Sreekanth, Tettey, Jun, & Mook, 2014), hexagonal (Sagar Raut & Thorat, 2015), or rectangular (Khatami, Alijani, Heli, & Sharifi, 2018). These morphological variations arise due to differences in preparation conditions, such as extract concentration, pH, and calcination temperature (Xu et al., 2021).The 8 %-ZnO/CQD nanocomposite features spherical CQDs wrapped onto rectangular ZnO NPs, similar to the morphology observed in 2 %-ZnO/CQD and 4 %-ZnO/CQD (Fig. S1). The lattice spacing s of 0.22 nm and 0.36 nm match the hexagonal (110) and graphite (0 0 2) planes (Hallam et al., 2021; Kaur et al., 2024; Song, Mitsuishi, Takeguchi, & Furuya, 2005), indicating that the spherical particles are CQDs, while the rectangular ones are ZnO. Together, these observations confirm the successful formation of the ZnO/CQD nanocomposite.

The surface chemical compositions of CQDs, ZnO, and 8 %-ZnO/CQD were analyzed using XPS. Fig. 2 a shows the full spectrum of CQDs, with C, O, and N as the major elements, while Zn, O, and C dominate the spectra of ZnO (Fig. 2b) and 8 %-ZnO/CQD (Fig. 2c). Peaks at 1021 and 1044 eV in ZnO and 8 %-ZnO/CQD (Fig. S2c and f) indicate Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 splitting, confirming Zn in the Zn2 + state. The O1s spectrum of CQDs shows peaks at 532 and 533 eV, corresponding to (C-O–H or C-O-C) and O = C-O bonds (Fig. S2a). For ZnO and 8 %-ZnO/CQD, O1s spectra were fitted with three peaks at 530, 531, and 532 eV (Fig. S2d and g) attributed to −O-Zn-O-, CO, and (C–O–H or C–O–C) bonds, respectively, similarly observed for 2 %-ZnO/CQD and 4 %-ZnO/CQD (Fig. S2k and o). The C1s spectrum of CQDs displays peaks at 288, 285, and 284 eV, indicating OC–O, (CC/C–C), and C–C bonds (Fig. S2b). Both ZnO and 8 %-ZnO/CQD show C1s peaks at 288, 286, and 285 eV (Fig. S2e and h), attributed to COOR, C–C, and CC/C–C bonds, respectively (Liang, Tai, Du, & Yin, 2020; Qu et al., 2020). Additionally, a π-plasmon peak at 292 eV is present only in ZnO (Fig. S2e), associated with π-π* shake-up transitions in aromatic ring structures in the C1s spectrum (Gengenbach, Major, Linford, & Easton, 2021; Jin et al., 2010). This results from electrostatic interactions between CQDs and ZnO, which alter π-π* transitions by refilling or depleting the CQD valence band when CQDs wrap ZnO (Kazeminava et al., 2022).

Raman and XRD analyses were performed to assess the structural and crystal morphological characteristics of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) (Fig. 3 a–b). In Fig. 3a, all spectra showed five Raman peaks at frequencies of 322, 438, 570, 1340, and 1588 cm−1. The sharp and strong peak at 438 cm−1 was attributed to the E2 (high) mode of ZnO, characteristic of the space group P63mc symmetry, which is indicative of a wurtzite structure (Huang, Kuo, & Shen, 2017). This peak originates from the vibration of oxygen in ZnO, indicating high crystal quality (Abdelouhab, Djouadi, Chelouche, & Touam, 2020). All nanocomposites showed peaks at 1340 and 1587 cm−1 (Fig. 3a), known as the D band and G band, which could evaluate the effect of wrapping ZnO with CQDs. Peaks observed in x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) demonstrated sharpness and higher intensity within the D and G bands compared to ZnO due to the presence of CQDs (Dychalska et al., 2015; Sharma, Singh, Dhar, Gondorf, & Spasova, 2012). These observations imply interconnected Raman activation modes between CQDs and ZnO in the x%-ZnO/CQD nanocomposites, indicating that CQDs were successfully wrapped around ZnO (Thyda et al., 2023).

Fig. 3b presents XRD pattern which indicated as the hexagonal wurtzite structure. Twelve primary characteristic diffraction peaks of ZnO were clearly observable, each corresponding to distinct crystal planes (1 0 0), (0 0 2), (1 0 1), (1 0 2), (1 1 0), (1 0 3), (1 1 2), (2 0 1), (2 0 3), (2 1 0), (2 1 1), and (1 1 4). Notably, additional heterogeneous diffraction peaks were not present, and the diffraction data were consistent with the standard XRD pattern of pure ZnO (JCPDS card No. 36-1451), indicating high purity with negligible impurities. Similar diffraction peaks were observed for x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) and ZnO, suggesting that the presence of CQDs did not affect the ZnO crystal structure during the wrapping process. Fig. S3 shows the XRD spectrum of CQDs, with a broad peak at 2θ = 23.5°, indicating the (0 0 2) plane of the graphitic structure and the amorphous nature of the CQDs(Alamdari, Almasi, Moradi, & Akhgari, 2023). The absence of CQD peaks in the XRD pattern of x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) (Fig. 3b) was attributed to the low CQD content. Raman analysis further confirmed the formation of ZnO/CQD nanocomposites (Fig. 3a) (Kumar, Yasoda, Kumaresan, Kothurkar, & Batabyal, 2018).

Fig. 3c presents the thermal properties of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The TGA curves showed a gradual mass decrease, with ZnO losing the most mass and 8 %-ZnO/CQD the least (Fig. 3c). Weight loss can be attributed to the evaporation of water, removal of residual organic matter, and decomposition of intermediate compounds of nanocomposites, including the removal of hydroxyl groups (Kayani, Anjum, Riaz, Naseem, & Zeeshan, 2020; Kayani, Manzoor, et al., 2020). Based on the TGA curves, x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) exhibited higher thermal stability than ZnO, possibly because CQDs provided greater thermal stability (Dua et al., 2023), and formed a dense, non-flammable carbon layer on the surface of ZnO, which effectively blocked contact between ZnO and air (Ren et al., 2023). This suggests that CQDs combined with ZnO, enhancing its structural stability.

Based on the characterization results of CQDs, ZnO, and x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10), it was confirmed that ZnO was well-wrapped in CQDs, indicating the successful synthesis of ZnO/CQD nanocomposites.

The results of the photocatalytic antimicrobial activities of the nanocomposites under 405 nm blue light are presented in Fig. 4 . For all tested pathogens (E. coli O157:H7, S. Typhimurium, L. monocytogenes), neither CQDs nor ZnO exhibited antimicrobial activity up to an exposure of 57.51 J/cm2 under 405 nm blue light. However, when ZnO was wrapped with CQDs, photocatalytic antimicrobial activity was observed (Fig. 4). This finding, that the photocatalytic activity of ZnO is enhanced when CQDs are applied to form a ZnO/CQD nanocomposite, has also been reported in other studies (Bazazi et al., 2023; Liang et al., 2020; Mandal et al., 2021; Muthulingam et al., 2015). The photocatalytic antimicrobial activities of ZnO/CQD nanocomposites varied depending on the CQD wrapping ratio and type, indicating that it is important to determine the optimal ratio for each synthetic material.

As shown in Fig. 4, the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity improved as the wrapping ratio increased, but when the wrapping ratio reached 10 %, the activity decreased. This finding suggests that the optimal ratio of CQDs to ZnO in our synthesized nanocomposite was 8 %. The photocatalytic activity of ZnO/CQD increased with a CQD ratio up to 8 % but decreased at 10 %, likely because excessive CQD decoration covered too much of the ZnO surface, blocking incident radiation and thereby reducing the generation of electron-hole pairs, ultimately diminishing the photocatalytic activity of the ZnO/CQD nanocomposite (Liang et al., 2020; Shafique, Mahr, Yaseen, & Bhatti, 2022).

The dark control, consisting of nanocomposites without 405 nm blue light treatment, was evaluated for surviving populations at 10-minute intervals over a 30-minute period, the maximum treatment duration of 405 nm blue light in this experiment. There was no significant change in the number of surviving individuals over time during the dark treatment process (p > 0.05) (Fig. 4b, d, and f). Therefore, it was evident that the antimicrobial activity of the x%-ZnO/CQD nanocomposites resulted solely from photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy band diagram and photocatalytic process of ZnO/CQD nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: We explored the charge transfer mechanism in ZnO/CQD nanocomposites by examining their band structures using UPS and Tauc plot (Fig. 5 ). The work functions (Φ) of CQDs and ZnO were calculated by subtracting their secondary cutoff binding energies (Ecutoff) from the incident photon energy in UPS (21.22 eV), resulting in 8.09 and 6.72 eV (versus vacuum level), respectively. Using the onset binding energy (Eonset), the valence band maximum (VBM) of CQDs and ZnO were determined to be 3.33 eV and 0.88 eV below the Fermi level (versus vacuum level), respectively, leading to calculated VBM values of 11.42 eV for CQDs and 7.6 eV for ZnO.

Furthermore, the bandgap (Eg) energies of the CQDs and ZnO were determined from the UV–Vis- diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) through linear extrapolation of (αhv)2 versus hv graph using the Tauc equation (Tauc, 1970); αhv = a(hv-Eg)n, where A, α, h, and v are a constant, the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively, and n = 1/2 and 2 are for the direct and indirect allowed transitions, respectively. Using the Eg values, the conduction band minimum (CBM) of CQDs and ZnO were determined to be 8.05 and 4.88 eV (versus vacuum level), respectively. According to the reference standard, where 0 V versus the reversible hydrogen electrode is equivalent to −4.44 eV, all derived energy values (VBM, Ef, CBM) were converted to electrochemical potentials measured in volts (V versus the normal hydrogen electrode, NHE) (Li et al., 2022).

As depicted in Fig. 6 , the VBM, Ef, and CBM of CQDs were 6.98, 3.65, and 3.61 V (versus NHE), respectively, while those of ZnO were 3.16, 2.28, and 0.44 V (versus NHE). From the energy band diagram results, it can be inferred that the Eg of CQDs is 3.37 eV, which is larger than the photon energy of 405 nm blue light (3.06 eV), thus preventing the generation of excited electrons and holes. Conversely, ZnO possesses a bandgap of 2.72 eV, which is smaller than the photon energy of 405 nm blue light, enabling the effective generation of electron-hole pairs. This observation can explain the previous findings where CQDs exhibited no photocatalytic antimicrobial activity, while ZnO demonstrated activity (Fig. 4).

When ZnO and CQDs form a nanocomposite, the energy bands of CQDs bend upwards and those of ZnO bend downwards until the Ef of both materials reach equilibrium. Using UPS spectra, we determined the equilibrium Ef of the CQD-ZnO nanocomposite and then elucidated the bent energy bands of CQDs and ZnO (Fig. 5d–e). As a result, the equilibrium Ef of the ZnO/CQD nanocomposite was determined to be 2.86 V (versus NHE), with the VBM/CBM of CQDs and ZnO measured at 6.19/2.82 V and 3.74/1.02 V, respectively (versus NHE). Before contact, the VBM of ZnO was more negative than the CBM of CQDs; however, after contact, the CBM of CQDs became more negative than the VBM of ZnO. This shift facilitated the efficient movement of photoexcited electrons from ZnO to the CBM of CQDs upon exposure to 405-nm blue light, thereby effectively suppressing electron-hole recombination in ZnO.

To validate this change, we measured the photoluminescence (PL) lifetimes and spectra of ZnO and x%-ZnO/CQD. PL analysis showed decreased lifetimes and intensity as CQD ratios increased, confirming enhanced charge separation (Fig. 7 ). Therefore, this result indicates that CQDs effectively separated photoexcited electrons from ZnO, thereby enhancing the activation of electron-hole pairs. The result of a decrease in PL lifetime and intensity with increasing introduction of CQDs is a phenomenon commonly observed in many studies (Beura, Pachaiappan, & Paramasivam, 2021; Beura & Thangadurai, 2017; Chen et al., 2015; Mandal et al., 2019). Furthermore, this suppression of electron-hole recombination allowed more hydroxyl radicals to form, as VBM of ZnO is more positive than the •OH/H2O potential (+2.34 V versus NHE) (Wood, 1988). Conversely, CBM of CQDs being more positive than the O2/O2 − potential (−0.33 V versus NHE) (Wood, 1988), suggested that electrons acted directly on pathogenic bacteria rather than reducing O2 to O2 −.

To identify the reactive species responsible for photocatalytic inactivation of pathogenic bacteria, radical-trapping experiments were conducted using isopropanol, potassium dichromate, sodium oxalate, and Trion as scavengers for •OH, e-, h+, and O2 −, respectively (Jabbar, Ebrahim, & Ammar, 2022; Kang & Kang, 2021). As shown in Fig. 8 a, the addition of isopropanol and potassium dichromate dramatically reduced microbial inactivation, whereas sodium oxalate and Trion did not show a significant difference (p > 0.05) compared to conditions without scavengers. This indicates that •OH and e− are the main reactive species in the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of ZnO/CQD. ESR analysis was performed using DMPO and TEMPO as spin trapping agents to further elucidate the charge transfer mechanism (Fig. 8 b-d). DMPO–•OH and DMPO–O2 − were identified by their distinct peak patterns, with DMPO–•OH characterizing four narrow peaks (1:2:2:1 ratio) and DMPO–O2 − displaying four broad, uniform peaks (Das, Biswal, Martha, & Parida, 2011). The higher intensity of DMPO–•OH adduct peaks in 8 %-ZnO/CQD compared to ZnO alone confirmed that CQDs suppress electron-hole pair recombination, allowing more h+ to oxidize H2O and generate a greater amount of •OH radicals. Notably, Fig. 8c shows that O2 − was not detected, which supports the results from Fig. 8c that O2 − is not a significant reactive species. Similarly, TEMPO, a typical spin-label molecule with a stable triplet ESR spectrum, was employed to detect photogenerated electrons. TEMPO exhibits three characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 in its ESR spectrum, representing its stable triplet state. Upon reduction by photogenerated e−, TEMPO is converted to TEMPOH, an ESR-silent hydroxylamine, leading to a significant decrease in peak intensity and flattening of the spectrum (He et al., 2014). In Fig. 8d, the reduction in TEMPO peak intensity was more pronounced for 8 %-ZnO/CQD than for ZnO, confirming that CQDs enhance the generation of photogenerated e−. These results collectively demonstrate that •OH radicals and e− are the primary reactive species driving the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of ZnO/CQD. The incorporation of CQDs improves charge separation, increases the availability of reactive species, and enhances photocatalytic efficiency.

In addition to charge separation and transfer, a key factor contributing to the excellent photocatalytic activity of nanocomposites is a large specific surface area, which provides more active sites for interactions with target substances (Guo, Shi, Guan, Huang, & Liu, 2017; Pirsaheb, Asadi, Sillanpää, & Farhadian, 2018). CQDs are well-known for their large surface area and abundant active sites (Yu et al., 2016). Accordingly, the BET analysis showed that the specific surface areas of ZnO, 2 %-ZnO/CQD, 4 %-ZnO/CQD, and 8 %-ZnO/CQD were 19.69, 34.24, 37.48, and 39.17 m2/g, respectively (Table 1 ), These results indicate that the introduction of CQDs to ZnO significantly increases the surface area, a phenomenon observed in several studies (Di et al., 2015; Li et al., 2019; Phang & Tan, 2019). Moreover, the increase in surface area with higher CQD ratios up to 8 % correlated with enhanced photocatalytic antimicrobial activity, showing that surface area acts as an important factor in the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of ZnO/CQD nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cell membrane damage & intracellular reactive oxygen species (iROS)
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the enhanced photocatalytic microbial control mechanism of ZnO/CQD nanocomposites, which is attributed to efficient charge separation and the increased specific surface area resulting from CQD incorporation, we investigated oxidative damage in pathogenic bacteria. We assessed cell membrane damage and lipid peroxidation, both of which compromise cell integrity and lead to cell death through oxidative reactions (Kang, Kim, & Kang, 2024; Soni, Choi, & Brightwell, 2021). PI, which fluoresces upon entering damaged cells, was used to measure membrane damage, and DPPP, reacting with hydroperoxides to form fluorescent DPPP oxide, was used for lipid peroxidation (Takahashi, Shibata, & Niki, 2001; Yang, Xiang, & Xu, 2015).

Table 2 shows that both types of damage increased with higher CQD wrapping ratios. We also compared iROS levels using CM-H2DCFDA, a fluorescent probe that is oxidized by reactive species (Pasciu, Nieddu, Sotgiu, Baralla, & Berlinguer, 2023; Riley et al., 2021). As indicated in Table 2, iROS generation increased with higher CQD content for E. coli O157:H7, S. Typhimurium, and L. monocytogenes (p < 0.05). These findings suggest that the incorporation of CQDs improves both the surface area and charge efficiency, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of ZnO/CQD nanocomposites.

The reusability of catalysts is a critical consideration for industrial applications, particularly regarding economic aspects and practical utilization, as it directly impacts the overall cost of processing chemical reactions and the availability of catalysts (Bayramoğlu, Korkut, & Ergan, 2021; Demirci & Sahiner, 2014; Kamari & Ghiaci, 2016). Therefore, 8 %-ZnO/CQD with higher photocatalytic antimicrobial activity (Fig. 4) than other x%-ZnO/CQD (x = 2, 4, 6, 10) nanocomposites were selected and used repeatedly (five cycles) under 405 nm blue light to evaluate its reusability.

As shown in Fig. 9 8%-ZnO/CQD did not exhibit statistically significant changes (p > 0.05) in its photocatalytic antimicrobial activity over five cycles of treatment under 405 nm blue light at an exposure of 28.76 J/cm2. This result suggests a high reusability potential for 8 %-ZnO/CQD, affirming its efficiency in repeated applications. Moreover, 8 %-ZnO/CQD maintained its photocatalytic antimicrobial activity after multiple reaction cycles. Therefore, it is expected to be suitable for use in immobilized forms in various water treatment applications, including wastewater purification, filtration systems, water pipes, and even as a coating material for water storage tanks.

We successfully dual-utilized chestnut shells, a byproduct of food processing, to synthesize ZnO and CQDs and produced ZnO/CQD nanocomposites by wrapping CQDs onto ZnO. The ZnO/CQD nanocomposite exhibited enhanced photocatalytic antimicrobial activity compared to ZnO alone, with the highest activity observed at an 8 % CQD ratio.

The enhanced photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of the ZnO/CQD nanocomposite was attributed to the CQDs suppressing electron-hole recombination, which increased the generation of reactive species (such as •OH and e−) and expanded the specific surface area, thereby increasing the number of active sites.

Combining two different nanomaterials to form a composite is an effective strategy for enhancing photocatalytic activity. This study is particularly significant in presenting an economical and environmentally friendly synthesis method for forming heterogeneous nanocomposites from a single resource.

Furthermore, the developed ZnO/CQD nanocomposite demonstrated photocatalytic activity under visible light and exhibited excellent stability, suggesting that it can be used sustainably by utilizing solar energy while being applied as a coating on various substrates.

Although we simulated water contamination with pathogens, further research is needed to evaluate whether 8 %-ZnO/CQD maintains its photocatalytic antimicrobial activity under visible light treatment in industrial wastewater applications. ZnO and CQDs are widely recognized for their high biocompatibility. However, the ZnO, CQDs, and ZnO/CQD nanocomposites synthesized in our study were developed via a pioneering approach distinct from previous research, and thus comprehensive toxicity assessments should be conducted in future studies prior to their application in the food industry.

Gyumi Na: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jun-Won Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
